title: Chute Boxe Academy

* [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva") Middleweight Champion (UFC 2006–2013) 185 lb (84 kg; 13.2 st) * [Cris Cyborg](/wiki/Cris_Cyborg "Cris Cyborg") Featherweight Champion ([Strikeforce](/wiki/List_of_Strikeforce_champions#Women's_Featherweight_Championship "List of Strikeforce champions") (2009–2011), [Invicta FC](/wiki/Invicta_FC "Invicta FC") (2013–2016), UFC (2016–2018) 145 lb (66 kg; 10.4 st) * [Wanderlei Silva](/wiki/Wanderlei_Silva "Wanderlei Silva") Middleweight Champion (Pride 2001–2007) 205 lb (93 kg; 14.6 st) * [Mauricio Rua](/wiki/Mauricio_Rua "Mauricio Rua") Light Heavyweight Champion (UFC 2010) 205 lb (93 kg; 14.6 st) * [Murilo Rua](/wiki/Murilo_Rua "Murilo Rua") Middleweight Champion (Elite XC 2007) 185 lb (84 kg; 13.2 st) [Charles Oliveira](/wiki/Charles_Oliveira "Charles Oliveira") UFC Lightweight Champion (2021-2022)
[Curitiba, Paraná](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba"), Brazil   [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_California "Long Beach, California"), California, United States

(Brazilian Portuguese: [[ˈʃutʃi ˈbɔksi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese"); "Kick-Boxing") is a Brazilian martial arts academy. It opened as a [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") academy in 1978 in [Curitiba](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba"), Paraná, Brazil. Head trainer Rudimar Fedrigo later expanded the program in 1991 to include other aspects of modern [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts"), such as wrestling and submission grappling. By 1995, the Chute Boxe team was considered a prime training ground for [Vale Tudo](/wiki/Vale_Tudo "Vale Tudo") fighters.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In 2004, an American branch, Chute Boxe USA, was established in Los Angeles, California.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Muay Thai academy in Curitiba, Brazil

Chute Boxe Academy

| --- | --- |

| Founded by | Rudimar Fedrigo |

| Current titleholders | [Cris Cyborg](/wiki/Cris_Cyborg "Cris Cyborg") Bellator Featherweight Champion (2020–present),   [Charles Oliveira](/wiki/Charles_Oliveira "Charles Oliveira") UFC BMF Champion (2026-Present) |
| Past titleholders | * [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva") Middleweight Champion (UFC 2006–2013) 185 lb (84 kg; 13.2 st) * [Cris Cyborg](/wiki/Cris_Cyborg "Cris Cyborg") Featherweight Champion ([Strikeforce](/wiki/List_of_Strikeforce_champions#Women's_Featherweight_Championship "List of Strikeforce champions") (2009–2011), [Invicta FC](/wiki/Invicta_FC "Invicta FC") (2013–2016), UFC (2016–2018) 145 lb (66 kg; 10.4 st) * [Wanderlei Silva](/wiki/Wanderlei_Silva "Wanderlei Silva") Middleweight Champion (Pride 2001–2007) 205 lb (93 kg; 14.6 st) * [Mauricio Rua](/wiki/Mauricio_Rua "Mauricio Rua") Light Heavyweight Champion (UFC 2010) 205 lb (93 kg; 14.6 st) * [Murilo Rua](/wiki/Murilo_Rua "Murilo Rua") Middleweight Champion (Elite XC 2007) 185 lb (84 kg; 13.2 st) [Charles Oliveira](/wiki/Charles_Oliveira "Charles Oliveira") UFC Lightweight Champion (2021-2022) |
| Training facilities | [Curitiba, Paraná](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba"), Brazil   [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_California "Long Beach, California"), California, United States |

The **Chute Boxe Academy** (Brazilian Portuguese: [[ˈʃutʃi ˈbɔksi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese"); "Kick-Boxing") is a Brazilian martial arts academy. It opened as a [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") academy in 1978 in [Curitiba](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba"), Paraná, Brazil. Head trainer Rudimar Fedrigo later expanded the program in 1991 to include other aspects of modern [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts"), such as wrestling and submission grappling. By 1995, the Chute Boxe team was considered a prime training ground for [Vale Tudo](/wiki/Vale_Tudo "Vale Tudo") fighters.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In 2004, an American branch, Chute Boxe USA, was established in Los Angeles, California.

Chute Boxe fighters are characterized by being extremely aggressive and physical, well-rounded fighters.[[2]](#cite_note-toddmartin-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chute_Boxe_Academy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chute_Boxe_Academy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings")]

In the 1970s, in search of a more full contact martial art, [Taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo") black belt [Nélio "Naja" Borges de Souza](/w/index.php?title=N%C3%A9lio_%22Naja%22_Borges_de_Souza&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nélio \"Naja\" Borges de Souza (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/N%C3%A9lio_Naja "pt:Nélio Naja")] learned Muay Thai in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") and brought it to Brazil. He moved to Curitiba and in 1978 his student Rudimar Fedrigo would found the Chute Boxing academy.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The style developed by Nélio Naja and his students had more emphasis on various kicks (due many of the early Brazilian Thai boxers coming from Taekwondo and [Kyokushin Karate](/wiki/Kyokushin_Karate "Kyokushin Karate")) but soon, the "Chute Boxe" style of Muay Thai became characterized as extremely aggressive, with wild combinations, hard low kicks and usually lacking in defense, trying to defeat their opponents as fast as possible.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The students of Nélio Naja also adopted a rank system of colored belt system used in other martial arts (in particular Taekwondo), in order to better rank and differentiate students, competitors, instructors and teachers, but soon adapted to Thai culture and switched to [*Prajieds*](/wiki/Pra_Jiad "Pra Jiad"), armband ropes traditionally wore by Thai boxers. Although not from Thailand, this system is widespread but not universal in Brazil.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The Chute Boxe team first made a name for themselves in the MMA world during the late 1990s in the Brazilian vale tudo promotion known as the [International Vale Tudo Championship (IVC)](/wiki/International_Vale_Tudo_Championship "International Vale Tudo Championship"). With an aggressive and physical style focused around their muay thai skills, Chute Boxe fighters captured three of the four title belts in the promotion ([Wanderlei Silva](/wiki/Wanderlei_Silva "Wanderlei Silva") winning the light heavyweight belt, José Landi-Jons winning the middleweight belt, and [Rafael Cordeiro](/wiki/Rafael_Cordeiro "Rafael Cordeiro") winning the lightweight belt). The promotion ended up serving as a spring board for the Chute Boxe team (as well as many other Brazilian MMA stars) into the lucrative Japanese MMA market. For Chute Boxe specifically, it would help to launch their careers in Japan's PRIDE FC.

### PRIDE FC dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chute_Boxe_Academy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: PRIDE FC dynasty")]

The major cog of the Chute Boxe machine in [PRIDE](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships") was [Wanderlei Silva](/wiki/Wanderlei_Silva "Wanderlei Silva"). Known for an exciting, brawling style complete with lethal knees and leaping stomps, he would exemplify Chute Boxe style martial arts in PRIDE's middleweight division and eventually win the [2003 PRIDE FC Middleweight Grand Prix](/wiki/PRIDE_Final_Conflict_2003 "PRIDE Final Conflict 2003") and the [PRIDE FC middleweight title](/wiki/List_of_PRIDE_champions "List of PRIDE champions") which he would hold for 5 1/2 years.

Coming off of a disappointing five-round decision loss to [Tito Ortiz](/wiki/Tito_Ortiz "Tito Ortiz") at [UFC 25: Ultimate Japan](/wiki/UFC_25 "UFC 25"), Silva would return to PRIDE to earn the biggest victory of his career to that point over the [Lion's Den's](/wiki/Lion%27s_Den_(mixed_martial_arts) "Lion's Den (mixed martial arts)") [Guy Mezger](/wiki/Guy_Mezger "Guy Mezger"). It would be 20 fights and over four years before he would lose again in a controversial decision to superheavyweight [Mark Hunt](/wiki/Mark_Hunt "Mark Hunt"). During this span he defeated notable fighters [Kazushi Sakuraba](/wiki/Kazushi_Sakuraba "Kazushi Sakuraba") (three times), [Quinton "Rampage" Jackson](/wiki/Rampage_Jackson "Rampage Jackson") (twice), [Yuki Kondo](/wiki/Yuki_Kondo "Yuki Kondo"), [Ikuhisa Minowa](/wiki/Ikuhisa_Minowa "Ikuhisa Minowa"), [Hidehiko Yoshida](/wiki/Hidehiko_Yoshida "Hidehiko Yoshida"), [Kiyoshi Tamura](/wiki/Kiyoshi_Tamura "Kiyoshi Tamura"), [Dan Henderson](/wiki/Dan_Henderson "Dan Henderson"), [Alexander Otsuka](/wiki/Alexander_Otsuka "Alexander Otsuka"), [Shungo Oyama](/wiki/Shungo_Oyama "Shungo Oyama"), [Hiromitsu Kanehara](/wiki/Hiromitsu_Kanehara "Hiromitsu Kanehara"), and would draw with [Mirko "Cro Cop" Filipović](/wiki/Mirko_Filipovi%C4%87 "Mirko Filipović").

His aura of invincibility wouldn't truly be broken until a decision loss to [Ricardo Arona](/wiki/Ricardo_Arona "Ricardo Arona") in the [2005 PRIDE FC middle weight Grand Prix](/wiki/PRIDE_Final_Conflict_2005 "PRIDE Final Conflict 2005"). His loss would be avenged later that night by teammate and rising star, Mauricio "Shogun" Rua. Silva is noted to have never lost under full PRIDE FC rules at his weight class; PRIDE's event in Las Vegas had altered rules. Silva Challenged Arona to a rematch for the middleweight title and beat him by decision.

### Enter the Rua brothers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chute_Boxe_Academy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Enter the Rua brothers")]

As the older of the Rua brothers, Murilo "Ninja" Rua had a mediocre run during his years with PRIDE FC. Wins over [Mario Sperry](/wiki/Mario_Sperry "Mario Sperry"), [Akira Shoji](/wiki/Akira_Shoji "Akira Shoji") and [Alexander Otsuka](/wiki/Alexander_Otsuka "Alexander Otsuka") established him as a contender in the organization. His younger brother Mauricio "Shogun" Rua would also enter the PRIDE fighting championships and would prove the most successful fighter produced by Chute Boxe under Wanderlei Silva's and Ninja's guidance.

Younger than Murilo by about a year and a half, "Shogun" has defeated a who's who list of fighters including [Evangelista "Cyborg" Santos](/wiki/Evangelista_Santos "Evangelista Santos"), Akira Shoji, [Akihiro Gono](/wiki/Akihiro_Gono "Akihiro Gono"), [Quinton "Rampage" Jackson](/wiki/Quinton_%22Rampage%22_Jackson "Quinton \"Rampage\" Jackson"), [Alistair Overeem](/wiki/Alistair_Overeem "Alistair Overeem") (twice), [Antônio Rogério "Minotouro" Nogueira](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Rog%C3%A9rio_Nogueira "Antônio Rogério Nogueira"), [Ricardo Arona](/wiki/Ricardo_Arona "Ricardo Arona"), [Kevin Randleman](/wiki/Kevin_Randleman "Kevin Randleman"), [Mark Coleman](/wiki/Mark_Coleman "Mark Coleman"), [Chuck Liddell](/wiki/Chuck_Liddell "Chuck Liddell"), and [Lyoto Machida](/wiki/Lyoto_Machida "Lyoto Machida"). Shogun was the UFC light heavyweight champion until losing his first title defense against Jon Jones at UFC 128. His exciting, fan-friendly style includes much of the standard Chute Boxe Muay Thai clinch work, knees, stomps, and soccer kicks, as well as polished [Brazilian Jiu Jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_Jiu_Jitsu "Brazilian Jiu Jitsu").

### Rivalry with Brazilian Top Team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chute_Boxe_Academy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rivalry with Brazilian Top Team")]

The opposite number in terms of Brazilian dominance in PRIDE FC was the [Brazilian Top Team](/wiki/Brazilian_Top_Team "Brazilian Top Team") (BTT) which was comprised, at the time, of such fighters as [Antonio Rodrigo "Minotauro" Nogueira](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Rodrigo_Nogueira "Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira"), [Ricardo Arona](/wiki/Ricardo_Arona "Ricardo Arona"), and [Murilo Bustamante](/wiki/Murilo_Bustamante "Murilo Bustamante"). Both BTT and Chute Boxe were the dominant Brazilian teams at the time, representing different contrasting combat philosophies: Chute Boxe was mostly based in a very aggressive Muay Thai style, while BTT was founded by [Carlson Gracie](/wiki/Carlson_Gracie "Carlson Gracie")'s students and had a more well-rounded BJJ-based approach. While there were some tensions between the team, the rivalry was thought ended after a discussion-turned-brawl between Ricardo Arona and Wanderlei Silva at the Tokyo Hilton hotel in the eve of [Pride 20](/wiki/Pride_20 "Pride 20") in 2002. The same event saw the first confrontation between both teams, matching Chute Boxe's representative Murílo "Ninja" Rua against BTT's [Mário Sperry](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Sperry "Mário Sperry").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Competition between the two teams was hardly limited to the Pride scene however. Matches between fighters of the two organizations had taken place in Brazil, Portugal and other parts of the world before, but perhaps the most interesting chapter of the rivalry took place on 28 August 2005 at the [2005 PRIDE FC middle weight Grand Prix](/wiki/PRIDE_Final_Conflict_2005 "PRIDE Final Conflict 2005").

The 2005 PRIDE FC middleweight Grand Prix would be the first such competition for newcomers Mauricio Rua and BTT's Ricardo Arona. Both scored impressive victories over highly regarded veterans Alistair Overeem and middleweight champ Wanderlei Silva respectively. It was Arona's victory over the latter that would stoke the flames of the rivalry because until then Silva had been the torch-bearer for Chute Boxe. Later that night, however, "Shogun" would pick up the torch with a thoroughly dominating first-round KO of Arona, ultimately finishing him on the ground which was considered Arona's strength.

Another notable aspect of this period is that it shortly followed the departure of promising prospect [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva"). Silva had noted several disputes with Chute Boxe's management as reasons for leaving the team and shortly began training with some of Brazilian Top Team's best in the Nogueira brothers. He was quoted in an [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") article as stating that friendships formed with his former rivals helped to save his career upon his departure from Chute Boxe.
[[9]](#cite_note-espnasilva-9)

### Post-Pride era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chute_Boxe_Academy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-Pride era")]

With the demise of PRIDE FC in late 2007, coinciding with the departure of its first mass-appeal superstar Wanderlei Silva around the same time, Chute Boxe entered a new era. Moreover, the Rua brothers, Mauricio and Murilo, and Andre "Dida" Amade, also departed Chute Boxe to start their own gym. One of the top instructors, [Rafael Cordeiro](/wiki/Rafael_Cordeiro "Rafael Cordeiro"), also left around that time to found his own gym in the US, [Kings MMA](/wiki/Kings_MMA "Kings MMA").[[10]](#cite_note-10)
However, up and coming fighters, such as lightweight Jean Silva, have augmented their talent. In addition, fighter [Evangelista Santos](/wiki/Evangelista_Santos "Evangelista Santos") has recently competed in [Strikeforce](/wiki/Strikeforce_(mixed_martial_arts) "Strikeforce (mixed martial arts)") as well as his ex-wife, female fighter [Cristiane "Cyborg" Santos](/wiki/Cristiane_Santos "Cristiane Santos"), who is currently Chute Boxe's most marketable fighter.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2018 UFC fighter [Charles "do Bronx" Oliveira](/wiki/Charles_Oliveira "Charles Oliveira") joined Chute Boxe Diego Lima in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo")[[12]](#cite_note-12) starting a 10-fight win streak.[[13]](#cite_note-13) According to him, his former gym Macaco Gold Team (headed by [Jorge "Macaco" Patino](/wiki/Jorge_Patino "Jorge Patino"), a Chute Boxe alumni) was mostly focused BJJ with complementary striking, and he felt he needed to improve his striking game. Patino is still his BJJ coach but now he is complemented with Chute Boxe's aggressive Muay Thai style.[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 2021 he became the [UFC Lightweight Champion](/wiki/UFC_Lightweight_Champion "UFC Lightweight Champion") after defeating [Michael Chandler](/wiki/Michael_Chandler "Michael Chandler") with a T.K.O.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 2023, Oliveira was awarded a black [prajied](/wiki/Pra_Jiad "Pra Jiad") in Muay Thai by Rudimar Fedrigo.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Chute Boxe fighters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chute_Boxe_Academy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Chute Boxe fighters")]

* [Wanderlei Silva](/wiki/Wanderlei_Silva "Wanderlei Silva") (former [PRIDE](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships") Middleweight Champion), left Chute Boxe in 2007 to train with [Xtreme Couture](/wiki/Xtreme_Couture "Xtreme Couture"). In 2008 Silva opened a new gym under the banner "[Wand Fight Team](/wiki/Wand_Fight_Team "Wand Fight Team")".[[17]](#cite_note-tatame-17)
* [Mauricio Rua](/wiki/Mauricio_Rua "Mauricio Rua") ([PRIDE](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships") 2005 Middleweight Grand Prix champion and former UFC champion light heavyweight), left Chute Boxe in 2007 and founded Universidade da Luta.
* [Murilo Rua](/wiki/Murilo_Rua "Murilo Rua") (former [Elite XC](/wiki/Elite_XC "Elite XC") Middleweight Champion), left Chute Boxe in 2007 and founded Universidade da Luta.
* [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva") (former UFC middleweight champion), left Chute Boxe in 2003 to start MuayThai Dream Team. Left MuayThai Dream Team to start at [Black House](/wiki/Black_House_(MMA) "Black House (MMA)").
* [Gabriel Gonzaga](/wiki/Gabriel_Gonzaga "Gabriel Gonzaga"), left Chute Boxe in 2006 to move to Ludlow, Massachusetts, to join Team Link.
* [Assuério Silva](/wiki/Assu%C3%A9rio_Silva "Assuério Silva"), left Chute Boxe in 2003 to start MuayThai Dream Team.
* [Thiago Silva](/wiki/Thiago_Silva_(fighter) "Thiago Silva (fighter)"), left Chute Boxe in 2008 and joined [American Top Team](/wiki/American_Top_Team "American Top Team").
* [Kazushi Sakuraba](/wiki/Kazushi_Sakuraba "Kazushi Sakuraba"), trained with former rival Wanderlei Silva at Chute Boxe in preparation for his bout with [Ken Shamrock](/wiki/Ken_Shamrock "Ken Shamrock") in 2005.
* [Andre Dida](/wiki/Andre_Dida "Andre Dida"), left Chute Boxe in 2007 and founded Universidade da Luta.
* [Cristiano Marcello](/wiki/Cristiano_Marcello "Cristiano Marcello"), left Chute Boxe in 2009 to focus on with his own martial arts academy, CM System.
* [Jorge Patino](/wiki/Jorge_Patino "Jorge Patino"), started Gold Team Fighters USA/Gold Team Fighters Houston.
* [Mariusz Linke](/wiki/Mariusz_Linke "Mariusz Linke"), first Polish born BJJ black belt, briefly trained with Cristiano Marcello and Jorge Patino.
* [Luiz Azeredo](/wiki/Luiz_Azeredo "Luiz Azeredo"), former [PRIDE FC](/wiki/PRIDE_FC "PRIDE FC") Lightweight title challenger.
* [Rafael Cordeiro](/wiki/Rafael_Cordeiro "Rafael Cordeiro"), left Chute Boxe in 2007 and founded [Kings MMA](/wiki/Kings_MMA "Kings MMA").

"Culturalmente muitos países utilizam o sistema de graduação nos Kruangs, o Brasil é um deles, desde sua criação em 1979 foi desenvolvida uma graduação para diferenciar os alunos, competidores, instrutores e professores, na época faixa na cintura e anos depois trocado para o Kruang. Diferente da Tailândia onde não existe academias de Muay Thai onde treinam pessoas comuns, somente existem campos de treinos para atletas." [Culturally, many countries use the Kruang grading system, and Brazil is one of them. Since its inception in 1979, a grading system has been developed to differentiate students, competitors, instructors, and teachers. At the time, it was a waist belt, but years later it was changed to the Kruang. Unlike Thailand, where there are no Muay Thai gyms for ordinary people to train, there are only training camps for athletes.]
